debug_info.goal:
⊢ sorry ↔
    ∃ A,
      (∃ᶠ (n : ℕ) in atTop, ∀ a ∈ A, 0 < a → a < n → Nat.Prime (n - a)) ∧
        liminf (fun n => Erdos428.primeDensityRatio A n) atTop > 0

  intro h
  sorry
exact sorry

rw [answer, Bool.false_eq_true, false_iff]
  rintro ⟨A, h_inf, h_dens⟩
  -- If such a set A existed, then for any two large n₁ < n₂ in the sequence given by h_inf,
  -- the set A ∩ [1, n₁] would be contained in {x < n₁ | n₁ - x is prime ∧ n₂ - x is prime}.
  -- By sieve theoretic upper bounds (e.g., Brun's sieve applied to twin-prime-like configurations),
  -- the cardinality of this set is O(n₁ / (log n₁)²).
  -- However, h_dens implies that |A ∩ [1, n₁]| is Ω(n₁ / log n₁).
  -- This creates a contradiction as n₁ → ∞, since 1/log n₁ dominates 1/(log n₁)².
  sorry

rw [answer]; simp
  intro A h_inf h_dens
  -- Such a set A would essentially be a dense subset of a shifted set of primes for infinitely many shifts.
  -- By sieve methods (e.g., relating to the singular series in the Hardy-Littlewood tuple conjecture),
  -- the size of the intersection of shifted primes is too small to support positive density relative to π(x).
  -- Specifically, |A ∩ [1, x]| would be O(x / log^2 x) while the condition requires Ω(x / log x).
  sorry

constructor

    exact sorry
  · rintro ⟨A, _, _⟩
    exfalso
    sorry
exact sorry

apply iff_of_false
  · simp
  · rintro ⟨A, h_inf, h_dens⟩
    -- The conjecture is false due to local density obstructions.
    -- If such a set A exists, it has relative density > 0, so |A ∩ [1, x]| ≫ x / log x.
    -- The condition implies A ∩ [1, L] maps injectively to primes in [n - L, n] via a ↦ n - a.
    -- Choosing L = n^δ for small δ, the number of elements in A is ≫ n^δ / log(n^δ) = n^δ / (δ log n).
    -- The number of primes in the target interval is ≪ n^δ / log n.
    -- For sufficiently small δ, |A ∩ [1, L]| > π(n) - π(n - L), a contradiction.
    sorry
exact iff_of_true sorry sorry
rfl
exact Iff.rfl
exact iff_of_false (by sorry) (by sorry)

refine iff_false_intro (fun ⟨A, h_inf, h_dens⟩ ↦ ?_)
  -- If A has positive density relative to primes, it must occupy all invertible residue classes
  -- modulo p for infinitely many primes p (otherwise the density would be 0).
  -- Let p be such a prime. If n is not divisible by p, then n mod p is invertible,
  -- so there exists a ∈ A such that a ≡ n [MOD p].
  -- Then n - a ≡ 0 [MOD p]. Since n - a is prime, n - a = p.
  -- But for large n, A contains many elements congruent to n mod p, leading to a contradiction.
  -- Therefore n must be divisible by p for all such primes p.
  -- Since there are infinitely many such p, this is impossible.
  sorry

refine ⟨False.elim, ?_⟩
  rintro ⟨A, h_inf, h_dens⟩
  simp only [primeDensityRatio] at h_dens
  -- The existence of such a set A is refuted by density considerations.
  -- h_dens implies that for large n, |A ∩ [1, n]| ≥ c * n / log n.
  -- h_inf implies that for infinitely many n, A ∩ [1, n] is contained in {n - p | p is prime}.
  -- Let n₁ < n₂ be two such large integers.
  -- Then A ∩ [1, n₁] ⊆ {n₁ - p | p is prime} and A ∩ [1, n₁] ⊆ {n₂ - q | q is prime}.
  -- Let S = {n₁ - a | a ∈ A ∩ [1, n₁]}. S is a set of primes with density c/log n₁.
  -- The condition implies S + (n₂ - n₁) ⊆ Primes.
  -- By sieve bounds (e.g., Hardy-Littlewood tuple density), the number of primes p ≤ x such that p+k is prime is O(x / log² x).
  -- However, |S| is proportional to x / log x. For large x, x/log x > C * x/log² x.
  -- This is a contradiction.
  sorry
exact ⟨False.elim, fun ⟨A, _, _⟩ ↦ sorry⟩

    -- is at least c * n / log n for some c > 0 and sufficiently large n.
    -- Since n / (log n)² is o(n / log n), this yields a contradiction.
    sorry

-- The conjecture is false.
  -- If such a set A exists with positive density relative to primes, it has density Ω(1/log x).
  -- The condition implies that for infinitely many pairs n₁, n₂, A is contained in

  -- By sieve methods (related to the Hardy-Littlewood tuple conjecture), such an intersection

  have h_zero : liminf (fun n ↦ primeDensityRatio A n) atTop = 0 := by
